sections.title: Use
rows: To report what another person said without using their exact words
Lena said that she had finished her homework.

sections.title: Form: tense changes
rows:
present simple
'I like pizza,' Tom said.

past simple
Tom said he liked pizza.

present continuous
'We are watching a film,' they said.

past continuous
They said they were watching a film.

present perfect
'I have seen that film,' Anna said.

past perfect
Anna said she had seen that film.

past simple
'I bought a new phone,' Jake said.

past perfect
Jake said he had bought a new phone.

will
'I will help you,' Sarah said.

would
Sarah said she would help me.

can
'I can swim well,' Leo said.

could
Leo said he could swim well.

must
'You must study harder,' the teacher said.

had to
The teacher said we had to study harder.

may
'I may travel abroad,' Nina said.

might
Nina said she might travel abroad.

exercises.title: A: Change to reported speech
exercises.instructions: Rewrite each sentence in reported speech.
exercises.studentInstruction: Rewrite each sentence in reported speech.

items.displayPrompt: 'I enjoy painting,' Kate said.

cues: 'I enjoy painting,' Kate said.

acceptedAnswers: Kate said she enjoyed painting.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Present simple, so the sentence is "Kate said she enjoyed painting.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: 'I enjoy painting,' Kate said. → Kate said she enjoyed painting.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Reported speech backshifts tenses and time/place words (now → then, tomorrow → the next day, here → there).

wrongOptions.Present: Check how "Present" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.
wrongOptions.simple: Check how "simple" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Direct speech in quotes becomes reported speech — backshift tenses and pronouns (I → he/she, we → they).

items.displayPrompt: 'We are cleaning the house,' they said.

cues: 'We are cleaning the house,' they said.

items.answer: They said they were cleaning the house.
acceptedAnswers: They said they were cleaning the house.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Present continuous, so the sentence is "They said they were cleaning the house.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: 'We are cleaning the house,' they said. → They said they were cleaning the house.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Reported speech backshifts tenses and time/place words (now → then, tomorrow → the next day, here → there).

wrongOptions.Present: Check how "Present" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.
wrongOptions.continuous: Check how "continuous" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Direct speech in quotes becomes reported speech — backshift tenses and pronouns (I → he/she, we → they).

items.displayPrompt: 'I can ride a horse,' Ben said.

cues: 'I can ride a horse,' Ben said.

items.answer: Ben said he could ride a horse.
acceptedAnswers: Ben said he could ride a horse.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Can, so the sentence is "Ben said he could ride a horse.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: 'I can ride a horse,' Ben said. → Ben said he could ride a horse.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Reported speech backshifts tenses and time/place words (now → then, tomorrow → the next day, here → there).

wrongOptions.Can: Check how "Can" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Direct speech in quotes becomes reported speech — backshift tenses and pronouns (I → he/she, we → they).

items.displayPrompt: 'They are going to travel abroad,' Mia said.

cues: 'They are going to travel abroad,' Mia said.

items.answer: Mia said they were going to travel abroad.
acceptedAnswers: Mia said they were going to travel abroad.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Are going to, so the sentence is "Mia said they were going to travel abroad.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: 'They are going to travel abroad,' Mia said. → Mia said they were going to travel abroad.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Reported speech backshifts tenses and time/place words (now → then, tomorrow → the next day, here → there).

wrongOptions.Are: Check how "Are" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.
wrongOptions.going: Check how "going" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Direct speech in quotes becomes reported speech — backshift tenses and pronouns (I → he/she, we → they).

items.displayPrompt: 'I may visit my cousins tomorrow,' Ella said.

cues: 'I may visit my cousins tomorrow,' Ella said.

items.answer: Ella said she might visit her cousins the next day.
acceptedAnswers: Ella said she might visit her cousins the next day.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: May, so the sentence is "Ella said she might visit her cousins the next day.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: 'I may visit my cousins tomorrow,' Ella said. → Ella said she might visit her cousins the next day.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Reported speech backshifts tenses and time/place words (now → then, tomorrow → the next day, here → there).

wrongOptions.May: Check how "May" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: In reported speech, change time and place words (tomorrow → the next day, here → there).
explanationContent.usefulTip: Direct speech in quotes becomes reported speech — backshift tenses and pronouns (I → he/she, we → they).

items.displayPrompt: 'I was studying all evening,' Jack said.

cues: 'I was studying all evening,' Jack said.

items.answer: Jack said he had been studying all evening.
acceptedAnswers: Jack said he had been studying all evening.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Past continuous, so the sentence is "Jack said he had been studying all evening.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: 'I was studying all evening,' Jack said. → Jack said he had been studying all evening.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Past perfect shows the earlier of two past actions — use had + past participle before the later past event.

wrongOptions.Past: Check how "Past" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.
wrongOptions.continuous: Check how "continuous" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Direct speech in quotes becomes reported speech — backshift tenses and pronouns (I → he/she, we → they).

exercises.title: B: Complete the reported sentences
exercises.instructions: Use the correct reported form of the verb in brackets.
exercises.studentInstruction: Use the correct reported form of the verb in brackets.

items.sentence: Ryan said he ...... (feel) tired after work.
acceptedAnswers: felt

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is present, so the correct answer is "felt".

wrongOptions.feel: The bare infinitive (feel) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (feel) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: My friends said they ...... (prepare) dinner then.
acceptedAnswers: were preparing

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is are preparing, so the correct answer is "were preparing".

wrongOptions.prepare: The bare infinitive (prepare) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (prepare) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: Olivia said she ...... (complete) the task already.
acceptedAnswers: had completed

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is present perfect, so the correct answer is "had completed".

wrongOptions.completed: Without had, the earlier-past relationship is lost.
wrongOptions.complete: The bare infinitive (complete) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple misses the link to now — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time words like already, yet, since, and for are strong signals for present perfect.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect (have/has + past participle) links past actions to now or unfinished time.

items.sentence: Daniel said he ...... (can) help us later.
acceptedAnswers: could

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is can, so the correct answer is "could".

wrongOptions.can: The bare infinitive (can) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals and semi-modals express ability, obligation, permission, or possibility — match the nuance in the sentence.

items.sentence: Mum said we ...... (have to) be careful.
acceptedAnswers: had to

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is have to, so the correct answer is "had to".

wrongOptions.to: Without had, the earlier-past relationship is lost.
wrongOptions.have to: The bare infinitive (have to) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals and semi-modals express ability, obligation, permission, or possibility — match the nuance in the sentence.

items.sentence: They said they ...... (travel) to Rome the following month.
acceptedAnswers: were going

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is future plan, so the correct answer is "were going".

wrongOptions.travel: The bare infinitive (travel) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Choose the future form that matches whether the sentence predicts, plans, or arranges.

exercises.title: C: Time and place changes
exercises.instructions: Rewrite with the correct time/place words.
exercises.studentInstruction: Rewrite with the correct time/place words.

items.displayPrompt: 'I'll see you tomorrow,' she said.

cues: 'I'll see you tomorrow,' she said.

items.answer: She said she would see me the next day.
acceptedAnswers: She said she would see me the next day.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Tomorrow, so the sentence is "She said she would see me the next day.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: 'I'll see you tomorrow,' she said. → She said she would see me the next day.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Reported speech backshifts tenses and time/place words (now → then, tomorrow → the next day, here → there).

wrongOptions.Tomorrow: Check how "Tomorrow" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: In reported speech, change time and place words (tomorrow → the next day, here → there).
explanationContent.usefulTip: Direct speech in quotes becomes reported speech — backshift tenses and pronouns (I → he/she, we → they).

items.displayPrompt: 'We arrived here yesterday,' they said.

cues: 'We arrived here yesterday,' they said.

items.answer: They said they had arrived there the day before.
acceptedAnswers: They said they had arrived there the day before.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Here, so the sentence is "They said they had arrived there the day before.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: 'We arrived here yesterday,' they said. → They said they had arrived there the day before.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Past perfect shows the earlier of two past actions — use had + past participle before the later past event.

wrongOptions.Here: Check how "Here" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: In reported speech, change time and place words (tomorrow → the next day, here → there).
explanationContent.usefulTip: Direct speech in quotes becomes reported speech — backshift tenses and pronouns (I → he/she, we → they).

items.displayPrompt: 'I'm busy now,' Luke said.

cues: 'I'm busy now,' Luke said.

items.answer: Luke said he was busy then.
acceptedAnswers: Luke said he was busy then.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Now, so the sentence is "Luke said he was busy then.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: 'I'm busy now,' Luke said. → Luke said he was busy then.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Reported speech backshifts tenses and time/place words (now → then, tomorrow → the next day, here → there).

wrongOptions.Now: Check how "Now" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: In reported speech, change time and place words (tomorrow → the next day, here → there).
explanationContent.usefulTip: Direct speech in quotes becomes reported speech — backshift tenses and pronouns (I → he/she, we → they).

items.displayPrompt: 'I met her last week,' Amy said.

cues: 'I met her last week,' Amy said.

items.answer: Amy said she had met her the week before.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Last week, so the sentence is "Amy said she had met her the week before.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: 'I met her last week,' Amy said. → Amy said she had met her the week before.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Past perfect shows the earlier of two past actions — use had + past participle before the later past event.

wrongOptions.Last: Check how "Last" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.
wrongOptions.week: Check how "week" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: In reported speech, change time and place words (tomorrow → the next day, here → there).
explanationContent.usefulTip: Direct speech in quotes becomes reported speech — backshift tenses and pronouns (I → he/she, we → they).

items.displayPrompt: 'I'll stay here tonight,' he said.

cues: 'I'll stay here tonight,' he said.

items.answer: He said he would stay there that night.
acceptedAnswers: He said he would stay there that night.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Here, so the sentence is "He said he would stay there that night.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: 'I'll stay here tonight,' he said. → He said he would stay there that night.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Reported speech backshifts tenses and time/place words (now → then, tomorrow → the next day, here → there).

wrongOptions.Here: Check how "Here" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: In reported speech, change time and place words (tomorrow → the next day, here → there).
explanationContent.usefulTip: Direct speech in quotes becomes reported speech — backshift tenses and pronouns (I → he/she, we → they).

items.displayPrompt: 'I started this job three years ago,' Paul said.

cues: 'I started this job three years ago,' Paul said.

items.answer: Paul said he had started that job three years before.
acceptedAnswers: Paul said he had started that job three years before.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: This job, so the sentence is "Paul said he had started that job three years before.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: 'I started this job three years ago,' Paul said. → Paul said he had started that job three years before.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Reported speech backshifts tenses and time/place words (now → then, tomorrow → the next day, here → there).

wrongOptions.This: Check how "This" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.
wrongOptions.job: Check how "job" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: In reported speech, change time and place words (tomorrow → the next day, here → there).
explanationContent.usefulTip: Direct speech in quotes becomes reported speech — backshift tenses and pronouns (I → he/she, we → they).

exercises.title: D: Choose the correct option
exercises.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.
exercises.studentInstruction: Tap the correct word or phrase.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "was" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "was" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "is" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.is: "is" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("hungry.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: They said they **can / could** join us later.
items.completedSentence: They said they could join us later.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Could softens the request or refers to past ability.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Can often gives permission or states ability. Could is more polite or past.

wrongOptions.can: Can may be too direct or present-tense for this context.
wrongOptions.could: Could may be too tentative when clear present permission is meant.

items.originalSentence: He said he **would / will** phone me that evening.
items.completedSentence: He said he would phone me that evening.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "would" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "would" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "will" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.will: "will" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("phone me that evening.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: Dad said we **have to / had to** leave early.
items.completedSentence: Dad said we had to leave early.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "had to" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "had to" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "have to" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.have to: "have to" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("leave early.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceBefore: Emma said she had seen him
items.originalSentence: Emma said she had seen him **yesterday / the day before**.
items.completedSentence: Emma said she had seen him the day before .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "the day before" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "the day before" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "yesterday" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.yesterday: "yesterday" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence (".").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceBefore: Tom said he might visit us
items.originalSentence: Tom said he might visit us **next month / the following month**.
items.completedSentence: Tom said he might visit us the following month .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "the following month" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "the following month" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "next month" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.next month: "next month" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence (".").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

exercises.title: E: Correct the errors
exercises.instructions: Each sentence has an error in reported speech. Write the whole sentence correctly.
exercises.studentInstruction: Each sentence has an error in reported speech. Write the whole sentence correctly.

items.displayPrompt: My uncle said he will buy a new car.

cues: My uncle said he will buy a new car.

items.answer: My uncle said he would buy a new car.
acceptedAnswers: My uncle said he would buy a new car.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Will.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: My uncle said he would buy a new car.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Reported speech backshifts tenses and time/place words (now → then, tomorrow → the next day, here → there).

wrongOptions.Will: Check how "Will" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.
explanationContent.question: My uncle said he <mistake>will</mistake> buy a new car.
explanationContent.correctedSentence: My uncle said he would buy a new car.

explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: She said they had to study for the test.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Reported speech backshifts tenses and time/place words (now → then, tomorrow → the next day, here → there).

wrongOptions.Have: Check how "Have" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.
wrongOptions.to: Check how "to" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.
explanationContent.question: She said they <mistake>have</mistake> to study for the test.
explanationContent.correctedSentence: She said they had to study for the test.

wrongOptions.past: Check how "past" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

wrongOptions.Are: Check how "Are" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

items.displayPrompt: Sophie said she may call me tomorrow.

cues: Sophie said she may call me tomorrow.

items.answer: Sophie said she might call me the next day.
acceptedAnswers: Sophie said she might call me the next day.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: May.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Sophie said she might call me the next day.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Reported speech backshifts tenses and time/place words (now → then, tomorrow → the next day, here → there).

wrongOptions.May: Check how "May" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: In reported speech, change time and place words (tomorrow → the next day, here → there).
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.
explanationContent.question: Sophie said she <mistake>may call me tomorrow.</mistake>
explanationContent.fix: "may call me tomorrow." → "might call me the next day." (use the correct quantifier or article)
explanationContent.correctedSentence: Sophie said she might call me the next day.

items.displayPrompt: My teacher said we have to finish the exercise.

cues: My teacher said we have to finish the exercise.

items.answer: My teacher said we had to finish the exercise.
acceptedAnswers: My teacher said we had to finish the exercise.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Have to.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: My teacher said we had to finish the exercise.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Build a grammatically complete sentence that matches the prompt: My teacher said we had to finish the exercise.

wrongOptions.Have: Check how "Have" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.
wrongOptions.to: Check how "to" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.
explanationContent.question: My teacher said we <mistake>have</mistake> to finish the exercise.
explanationContent.correctedSentence: My teacher said we had to finish the exercise.

items.displayPrompt: She said she would arrive tomorrow.

cues: She said she would arrive tomorrow.

items.answer: She said she would arrive the next day.
acceptedAnswers: She said she would arrive the next day.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Tomorrow.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: She said she would arrive the next day.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Reported speech backshifts tenses and time/place words (now → then, tomorrow → the next day, here → there).

wrongOptions.Tomorrow: Check how "Tomorrow" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: In reported speech, change time and place words (tomorrow → the next day, here → there).
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.
explanationContent.question: She said she would arrive <mistake>tomorrow.</mistake>
explanationContent.fix: "tomorrow." → "the next day." (use the correct quantifier or article)
explanationContent.correctedSentence: She said she would arrive the next day.

items.displayPrompt: Ben said he has done his homework already.

cues: Ben said he has done his homework already.

items.answer: Ben said he had done his homework already.
acceptedAnswers: Ben said he had done his homework already.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Present perfect.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Ben said he had done his homework already.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links past actions to now — has/have + past participle; yet goes at the end of negatives.

wrongOptions.Present: Check how "Present" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.
wrongOptions.perfect: Check how "perfect" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.
explanationContent.question: Ben said he <mistake>has</mistake> done his homework already.
explanationContent.correctedSentence: Ben said he had done his homework already.

exercises.title: G: Correct the errors
exercises.instructions: Each sentence has one mistake in reported speech. Find the bold word and write the correction.
exercises.studentInstruction: Each sentence has one mistake in reported speech. Find the bold word and write the correction.

items.sentence: Maria said she is tired after school.
acceptedAnswers: was

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The highlighted word breaks the collocation or meaning — choose the word that fits naturally in this context.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: The highlighted word breaks the collocation or meaning — choose the word that fits naturally in this context.

wrongOptions.is: "is" is the highlighted error — use "was" here instead.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The error is in "is", not elsewhere — replace it with "was" and read the full sentence to check it sounds natural.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.
explanationContent.question: Maria said she <mistake>is</mistake> tired after school.
explanationContent.correctedSentence: Maria said she was tired after school.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Maria said she was tired after school.

items.sentence: They said they will help us.
acceptedAnswers: would

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The highlighted word breaks the collocation or meaning — choose the word that fits naturally in this context.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: The highlighted word breaks the collocation or meaning — choose the word that fits naturally in this context.

wrongOptions.will: "will" is the highlighted error — use "would" here instead.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The error is in "will", not elsewhere — replace it with "would" and read the full sentence to check it sounds natural.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.
explanationContent.question: They said they <mistake>will</mistake> help us.
explanationContent.correctedSentence: They said they would help us.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: They said they would help us.

items.sentence: He said he can drive very well.
acceptedAnswers: could

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The highlighted word breaks the collocation or meaning — choose the word that fits naturally in this context.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: The highlighted word breaks the collocation or meaning — choose the word that fits naturally in this context.

wrongOptions.can: "can" is the highlighted error — use "could" here instead.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The error is in "can", not elsewhere — replace it with "could" and read the full sentence to check it sounds natural.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.
explanationContent.question: He said he <mistake>can</mistake> drive very well.
explanationContent.correctedSentence: He said he could drive very well.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: He said he could drive very well.

items.sentence: Dad said we have to be quiet.
acceptedAnswers: had to

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The highlighted word breaks the collocation or meaning — choose the word that fits naturally in this context.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: The highlighted word breaks the collocation or meaning — choose the word that fits naturally in this context.

wrongOptions.have to: "have to" is the highlighted error — use "had to" here instead.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The error is in "have to", not elsewhere — replace it with "had to" and read the full sentence to check it sounds natural.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.
explanationContent.question: Dad said we <mistake>have to</mistake> be quiet.
explanationContent.correctedSentence: Dad said we had to be quiet.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Dad said we had to be quiet.

items.sentence: She said she saw him yesterday.
acceptedAnswers: the day before

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

wrongOptions.yesterday: "yesterday" is the highlighted error — use "the day before" here instead.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The error is in "yesterday", not elsewhere — replace it with "the day before" and read the full sentence to check it sounds natural.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.
explanationContent.question: She said she saw him <mistake>yesterday</mistake>.
explanationContent.fix: "yesterday" → "the day before" (use the correct quantifier or article)
explanationContent.correctedSentence: She said she saw him the day before.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: She said she saw him the day before.

items.sentence: Tom said he has finished dinner.
acceptedAnswers: had

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Check tense and subject–verb agreement — the highlighted form must match the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Check tense and subject–verb agreement — the highlighted form must match the time clues in the sentence.

wrongOptions.has: "has" is the highlighted error — use "had" here instead.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The error is in "has", not elsewhere — replace it with "had" and read the full sentence to check it sounds natural.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.
explanationContent.question: Tom said he <mistake>has</mistake> finished dinner.
explanationContent.correctedSentence: Tom said he had finished dinner.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Tom said he had finished dinner.

exercises.title: H: Rephrasing
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence so it has a similar meaning to the first. Use between one and four words.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence so it has a similar meaning to the first. Use between one and four words.

acceptedAnswers: would help me

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is will help, so the correct answer is "would help me".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "would help me", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "would help me" is a phrasal verb or fixed chunk that collocates with the rest of the sentence.

items.sentence: They said they ...... for the test.
acceptedAnswers: were revising

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is are revising, so the correct answer is "were revising".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "were revising", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Tina said she ...... a new laptop the following year.
acceptedAnswers: might buy

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Might buy a laptop the following year.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

acceptedAnswers: had been reading

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past continuous, so the correct answer is "had been reading".

wrongOptions.been reading: Without had, the earlier-past relationship is lost — use past perfect for the first of two past actions.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "had been reading", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

acceptedAnswers: had cleaned

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is present perfect, so the correct answer is "had cleaned".

wrongOptions.cleaned: Without had, the earlier-past relationship is lost — use past perfect for the first of two past actions.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence: My friends said they ...... the next day.
acceptedAnswers: could meet

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Could meet the next day.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.
